title: VPN Kill Switch Explained: Why You Need One and How It Works
description: A VPN kill switch cuts your internet connection if the VPN drops. Without one, your real IP can leak for seconds before you notice. Here is what to look for.
content:
A VPN kill switch is a feature that automatically disconnects your device from the internet the moment the VPN connection drops. Without a kill switch, there is a window between when the VPN fails and when you notice, during which your traffic travels over your regular connection with your real IP address exposed. For most users this is an inconvenience. For journalists, activists, or people in countries with heavy surveillance, it is a serious risk.

How it works technically: the VPN client monitors the tunnel connection. When the tunnel drops, the kill switch triggers a firewall rule that blocks all outgoing traffic. This happens in milliseconds. When the VPN reconnects, the firewall rule is removed and normal traffic resumes.

There are two types: application-level and system-level. Application-level kill switches cut only specific apps (like your browser or torrent client) when the VPN drops. System-level kill switches cut all internet traffic on the device. System-level is more thorough but can be more disruptive if the VPN drops frequently.

Not all VPNs implement kill switches equally well. Some have kill switches that only trigger when the VPN software detects the disconnection, which can miss some failure modes. Better implementations work at the operating system's network filter level, which catches even force-killed VPN processes.

On mobile devices, the situation is more complex. Android's built-in Always-on VPN feature with the block connections without VPN setting is effectively a system-level kill switch that works independently of the VPN app. iOS has similar functionality. Using the operating system's native feature is more reliable than relying on the VPN app's own kill switch on mobile.

If privacy is important to your use case, test your VPN's kill switch before relying on it. Disconnect the VPN while running a DNS leak test and check whether any requests get through. A properly functioning kill switch should block everything the moment the VPN drops.
